coredata.dc:title: Potato starch derived N-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing tool for ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Preethi, M.
Murugan, R.
Viswanathan, C.
Ponpandian, N.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a specific group of nanocarbons, are garnering popularity as a potential optical sensing material due to their unique properties. In the current study, with the use of ultrasonication, potato starch is used as source forcarbonand ethylenediamine used asnitrogen source to generate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots in one phase. This method is both environmentally friendly and cost-effective. A considerable quantum yield (QY) of 49.26 % and exhibited a blue fluorescence was observed in the prepared N-CQDs. Under optimum conditions, ascorbic acid (AA) can be added to N-CQDs may promote aggregation and electron transfer due to the interaction between the hydrogen bonds in the N-CQDs and the AA, which may result in fluorescence quenching. With a limit of detection (0.093 µM),fluorescence quenching between N-CQDs and AA concentration, was found to be perfectly linear in the range 0–130 µM was observed. Moreover, a practical application for AA detection on vitamin C tablets was performed, and the outcomes revealed a tremendous recovery rate. As a result, this work provides a realistic possibility for deploying a vitamin C sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potato starch derived N-CQDs
Ultrasonication-assisted technique
Ascorbic acid detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of a PLQY for N-CQDs
Investigation on morphology and its structure for N-CQDs
Detection mechanism for AA using fluorescence sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for explaining the steps involved in generating N-CQDs from potato starch and demonstrating the PLsensing of vitamin C usingN-CQDs.
(a) HRTEM image depicts the homogeneous distribution of N-CQDs, (b) XPS spectral survey for N-CQDs, (c) Deconvoluted C1s spectrum for N-CQDs, (d) Deconvoluted N1s spectrum for N-CQDs.

(a) N-CQDs exhibit a peak with a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.7° (0.39 nm) on their XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectral analysis of N-CQDs were around 1358 cm−1 (D band) and 1598 cm−1 (G band), with an intensity ratio (ID/IG) for N-CQDs was found to be 1.12, indicating that the structural defects correlated with the amorphous phase. (c) The spectrum of FTIR for N-CQDs reveal oxygen functional groups on their surfaces, (d) An absorption peak was observed at 278 nm was found to be the transition of C=C bond (π-π*) and 347 nm of n-π* transition in N-CQDs which exhibits blue luminescence.

(a) The PL emission spectrum for N-CQDs with different wavelengths ranging from the 330 nm and 410 nm, and the highest PL intensity was examined at 350 nm with the emission wavelength was 435 nm. (b) A histogram was framed between themeasured fluorescence intensity and the wavelength at differentpH values (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) with pH = 7 having the highest intensity of PL when compared against other pH values. (c) The relationship between the emission and excitation wavelengths suggests that the produced N-CQDs displayed excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) emission. (d) The spectra of the N-CQD’s fluorescence at various AA concentrations range between 0 and 130 µM. The intensity of PL decreases when increasing the concentration of AA, which indicates that AA can quench the N-CQD’s fluorescence.

(a) A calibration graph demonstrates the perfect correlation coefficient of 0.9926 between the various relative strengths and the AA concentration under optimal conditions, (b) The interference investigation of N-CQDs in an existence of various amino acids at a concentration of 130 µM.

(a) The UV–vis spectra of AA reveals that it exhibits a significant absorption band at 285 nm, indicating that there is no overlap with the emission wavelength of the N-CQDs at 435 nm, (b) There is no overlap between these two excitation and emission spectra also eliminates the possibility of IFE, (c) Hydrogen bonding between N-CQDs and AA might lead to agglomeration of N-CQDs, which may lead to fluorescence quenching.
A comparison of analytical parameters of the proposed approach, such as linear range and limit of detection.
Recovery of Vitamin C in vitamin C tablet (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Potato starch derived N-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing tool for ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a specific group of nanocarbons, are garnering popularity as a potential optical sensing material due to their unique properties. In the current study, with the use of ultrasonication, potato starch is used as source forcarbonand ethylenediamine used asnitrogen source to generate nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots in one phase. This method is both environmentally friendly and cost-effective. A considerable quantum yield (QY) of 49.26 % and exhibited a blue fluorescence was observed in the prepared N-CQDs. Under optimum conditions, ascorbic acid (AA) can be added to N-CQDs may promote aggregation and electron transfer due to the interaction between the hydrogen bonds in the N-CQDs and the AA, which may result in fluorescence quenching. With a limit of detection (0.093 µM),fluorescence quenching between N-CQDs and AA concentration, was found to be perfectly linear in the range 0–130 µM was observed. Moreover, a practical application for AA detection on vitamin C tablets was performed, and the outcomes revealed a tremendous recovery rate. As a result, this work provides a realistic possibility for deploying a vitamin C sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: A crucial biomolecule, ascorbic acid (AA) is involved with the production of such collagen, norepinephrine, and neuronal hormones, and it is also involved in the metabolism of folic acid, tyrosine, and tryptophan [1]. Additionally, Ascorbic acid (vitamin C), is a water-soluble acid that is essential to human health and is associated with a variety of metabolic activities; its behaves as acofactor in a variety of enzyme, promotes intestinal iron absorption, which is linked in carnitine formation and cell metabolism [2]. This ascorbic acid cannotbe produced in the human body due to a lack of the enzyme (l-gulono-gamma-lactone oxidase), which is required for the production of vitamin C from D-glucose [3].

Vitamin C was an effective reducing agent that scavenges free radicals within biological systems due to their high anti-oxidant ability, resulting in free radical neutralization through hydrogen radical interaction. Ascorbate radical is generally inactive free radical due to their unpaired electron being strongly relocated in the π system, making this a superior biological donor anti-oxidant ascorbate [4]. Vitamin C can prevent cell damage generated by reactive oxygen species and damaging free radicals [5].

Vitamin C is found in various vegetables, including tomatoes, broccoli, peppers, cauliflower, brussels sprouts, and potato. However, AA is frequently added to juices and beverages as a flavouring agent. Excess AA consumption may result in diarrhoea, stomach bleeding, stone formation, anaemia, gout, and neonatal dependency. It is critical to examine the amount of AA we consume for our health. The pharmaceutical, healthcare, and food sectors focus on high sensitivity detection of AA. Electrochemical techniques [6], fluorescence [7], chemiluminescence [8], electrophoresis [9], and High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [10] are the most frequently used methods for ascorbic acid detection. Due to its flexibility of operation, high sensitivity, low cost, and simplicity, the fluorescence method is the most often used technique for ascorbic acid detection.

The researchers presented an environmentally friendly route of producing carbon quantum dots that makes use of natural and environmentally suitable precursors such as coconut water [11], curry berries [12], muskmelon peel [13], and other naturalmaterials. There are many approaches to prepare CQDs, including stirrer assisted technique [13], ultrasonication assisted technique [12], microwave assisted technique [14] etc. Among this, ultrasonication-assisted procedure has maintained their prominence because of its cost-effectiveness and simplicity. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) belong to the class of zero-dimensional carbon materials which are widely studied for various applications because of its unique physiochemical properties, non-toxic nature, environmental and biocompatibility, and low cost. They can be easily functionalized to be applicable for various applications such as catalysts, bioimaging agents, sensors, optical elements in opto-electronic devices and lasers.

Functionalization, which includes surface passivation and heteroatom doping, is an effective method of altering the properties of CQDs [15,16]. However, surface passivation is inefficient, and the majority of an organic chemicals or catalysts utilized were extremely dangerous to humans and the environment. In recent years, heteroatom doping of CQDs has attracted a great deal of attention [17]. However, because they have larger radii than carbon atoms, metal/transition metal atoms cause doping to be less effective and non-uniform in its distribution across the material. There are several drawbacks of doping metal atoms like toxicity, non-biodegradability and high cost. To overcome these drawbacks, heteroatoms such as N, P, S, B, doping can be done that enhances the fluorescence quantum yields and intrinsic features of CQDs by adjusting the carbon frameworks and their surface chemical structures.

However, highly selective, existing fluorescence methods are mostly based on quite expensive, require long time for analysisand require precise calibration. Also, preparation of quantum dots involves the usage of harmful chemicals. However, in our work, we have followed a green synthetic approach as a cost-effective, non-toxic, short reaction time and environmentally friendly method to develop the N-CQDs from potato starch with neutral pH, using ultrasonication-assisted technique. This sensor is non-toxic, cheap, and is made from potato starch that is easily available and is also ideal for large scale process. Besides, this sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity at a high concentration of AA.

In our study, we proposed a cost-effective, non-toxic, and ecologically acceptable method for developing N-CQDs from potato starch with neutral pH and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources respectively for the synthesis of N-CQDs. Globally, over 233 MTonnes of potatoes are produced, with the market for these vegetables rapidly booming. Food waste is created on an annual basis in the amount of 1.3 billion metric tonnes, with the majority of this waste not being disposed properly. The utilization of potato starch as a carbon source enables an upcycling value for food wastes and also reduces the cost of developing carbon nanomaterials. In particular, the prepared N-CQDs were employed as fluorescent probe to detect vitamin C with high sensitivity and selectivity. No investigations on the preparation of N-CQDs from potato starch to detect Vitamin C using photoluminescence quenching have been reported, thus far. Hence, in our report, we propose a biomass derived N-doped CQDs prepared using facile ultrasonication method and studied its application as a fluorescent for AA sensing.

The potatoes were obtained from a local supermarket (Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India). All the chemicals used during this investigation were analytical grade chemicals obtained from Himedia and Sigma-Aldrich and were used without further purification. All aqueous solution were synthesized utilizing DI water (deionized).

The N-CQDs were generated from potato starch in a single step employing an ultrasonication approach, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . To elaborate, raw potatoes were purchased from a supermarket and washed in DI water. The potato was sliced into small pieces and soaked in DI water (100 mL) for about 5 h; the starch in the potato settled down to the bottom of itscontainer. Finally, it was separated and dried overnightat room temperature. Using a stirrer, 1.5 g of potato starch and 2 mL of ethylenediamine were blended with 25 mL of distilled water; the mixture were sonicated for over 5 h. The solution turned from transparent to light brown as a result of the strong oxidation, and its precipitate were filtered throughWhatman filter paper. The solution was centrifuged at 12,500 rpm, and this supernatant was filtered using amembrane filter (0.22 μm) and a dialysis membrane (3500 MWCO). The final product was lyophilized before further usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of a PLQY for N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The PLQY (photoluminescence quantum yield) were measured through correlating the absorption of N-CQDs to a PL intensity, with quinine sulfate serving as reference materialfor the analysis. The equation for PLQY can be summarized as follows:
Q
Y S
= Q
Y
ST
·
I S
I
ST
·
η S 2
η
ST
2
·
A
ST
A S

N-CQDs are symbolized as “S,” while the reference (Quinine sulfate) is symbolized as “ST.” In this equation, “A” denotes the absorption of both reference material and N-CQDs, “I” represents the photoluminescence intensity of the reference material and N-CQDs, and “η” represents the refractive index of both reference and N-CQDs (both are 1.33). For N-CQDs, the standard reference material was Quinine sulphate in (0.1 M H2SO4) with a QYST of 54%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on morphology and its structure for N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure and morphology for N-CQDs were evaluated using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM). From the HRTEM images, this was observed that N-CQDs become spherical and widely dispersed. They also had uniform size, having an average diameter of 3.2 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). It could also be affirmed that majority of the nanoparticles are made up of amorphous carbon.

The chemical bonding and elemental composition for N-CQDs were examined using XPS measurements. The synthesized N-CQDs were mainly framed with carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, respectively (Fig. 2(b)). There are three distinct peaks in the spectral deconvolution of C1s: 284.21, 286.16, and 288.21 eV, which contribute to sp2 carbon (C–C/C=C), C–N, and C=O (Fig. 2(c)). A peak at 284.21 eV correspond with sp2 (C–C) and suggests that the abundance of graphite structures in the obtained N-CQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s revealed two peaks of 399.36 and 400.35 eV attributed to C–N–C and N–H, respectively (Fig. 2(d)). The high-resolution spectra for O1s revealed two peaks having binding energies of 530.32 and 532.46 eV, confirming that C–OH and O–H. These results demonstrate that nitrogen atom was effectively integrated into CQDs and that functional groups including C–N, C–C, O–H, and N–H co-existed over the surface of N-CQDs.

A wide diffraction peak of about 22.7° (d002 = 0.39 nm) suggests the occurrence of an amorphous carbon phase, as well as partial graphitization associated with the abundance of functional groups as well as the graphitization of carbon atoms (Fig. 3 (a)). These findings further indicate the existence of a graphite structure within the carbon core of N-CQDs.

The Raman spectrum confirms the crystalline and vibrational modes of N-CQDs (Fig. 3(b)). N-CQDs exhibit two unique peaks in their Raman spectrum: The D band features a peak at 1358 cm−1, while the G band peaks at 1598 cm−1. The D band (disorder) is related to disorder carbon atoms with dangling bond in a two-dimensional graphite lattice. In contrast, the G band (crystalline) is related with the sp2 linked carbon atoms. The D band (ID) and G band (IG) ratio (ID/IG) for N-CQDs was found to be 1.12, indicating that the structural defects correlated with the amorphous phase. A comprehensive analysis of Raman spectroscopy shows that the CQDs are mainly constituted of sp2 graphitic carbon and sp3 carbon defects.

The surface group for N-CQDs was determined by analyzing the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3(c)). There were broad stretching vibrations in the 3376 and 3219 cm−1 regions for the N–H and O–H bond vibrations. A vibrational band at 1167 cm−1 confirms the existence of primary O–H groups on the N-CQDs surface. A peaks around 2137 cm−1 and 1077–1305 cm−1 suggest the prevalence of aliphatic functional C–H and C–N/C–O groups. There is an N–H vibration and deformation band at 1508 cm−1. This suggests that there is an amino-functional group. The appearance of vibrational mode in the range 1638 cm−1 identified an existence for C=O. The prominent peak of 994 cm−1 is the process of oxygenation of C–H group. The FTIR observations indicate functional groups including amine, hydroxyl, and carbonyl on N-CQD’s surface. It can be inferred that the N-CQDs have hydrophilic groups on their surface, enabling them to be highly soluble in water. It is possible to conclude that the N-CQDs contain hydrophilic groups on the surface that allow them to be extremely easily soluble in water.

The UV–Vis spectra showed distinctive absorption peaks at 270 nm, ascribed to the C=C bond of π-π* transition (Fig. 3(d)). According to the former absorption peak, the electronic transition of aromatic sp2 domains creates delocalized states on the basal planes, comparable to graphene.

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of a prepared N-CQDs were observed utilizing a range of various excitation wavelengths. It is apparent in the PL spectrum that peak intensity of emission changes with corresponding excitation wavelength in the range of 330 to 410 nm; the maximum intensity of emission was detected at 350 nm. This corresponds to the emission wavelength of 435 nm (Fig. 4 (a)). If the excitation wavelength increases the emission wavelength have no noticeable change in wavelength. In accordance with Fig. 4(c), the synthesized N-CQDs exhibited excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) emission behaviour. The self-passivated nitrogen-containing CQDs may be the consequence of sequential dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and surface passivation with carbonaceous organic materials. The obvious difference in PL behavior between CQDs and N-CQDs was studied and is shown in Fig. S3. It demonstrates that the maximum emission wavelength of undoped CQDs is 375 nm, whereas the maximum emission wavelength of N-CQDs is 435 nm. Hence, nitrogen doping causes a red shift in fluorescence.

The existence of nitrogen as a doping elementplays an essential role in explaining the high fluorescence CQDs. The doped N atoms might induce an upward shift in the Fermi level. The QY of CQD has been demonstrated to be enhanced by nitrogen doping and surface functionalization with an amine group. A typical solution optimized with H2SO4 (0.1 M) at 350 nm resulted in a quantum yield of 49.26 % for N-CQDs, respectively. Quinine sulfate was employed as standard reference material to determine the QY of N-CQD in this investigation. The radiative recombination of excitons between the delocalized π orbital and the doped nitrogen atoms in N-CQDs results in a high PLQY of 49.26%, which is significantly more than the 21.46% in undoped CQDs. The mass of the N-CQDs (m) and the mass of the potato starch (M) were multiplied together to calculate the product yield (PY) with N-CQDs. A PY of produced N-CQDs of 30 mL was found to be 21.92 %. (PY = (m/M) × 100 %).

The Fig. S6 shows the effect of ratio of potato starch (PS) and ethylenediamine (EDA) on PL intensity of N-doped CQDs. When the quantity of potato starch increased from 0.5 g to 2.5 g, the PL intensity increased gradually and reaches maximum at the 1.5 g potato starch. However, when the concentration further increased, the PL intensity decreases, which indicated that the addition of excessive carbon source inhibits the formation of CQDs. Similarly, as for the quantity of EDA, the PL intensity gets maximum when the quantity of ethylenediamine is 2 mL. The PL intensity firstly increased with increasing the amount of PS: EDA, and then dramatically decreases at higher concentration of PS: EDA. The PL intensity decreases due to the saturation of the nitrogen atom that replaced the CQDs in the host lattice at higher concentration of PS: EDA. Hence, the mixing ratio of potato starch to ethylenediamine was optimized as 1.5:2.0 for the prepared N-doped CQDs to possess maximum PL intensity.

An influence on pH over fluorescence intensity for N-CQDs was only minimally significant (Fig. 4(b)). However, intensity of PL recorded in either an acidic or a basic solution was reduced. This could be because H+ ions or OH– ions create considerable modifications in the functional groups. This phenomenon could be related to the protonation (addition of H+ ions) or deprotonation (transfer of H+ ions) activities the functional groups that are located on the N-CQD's surface. Then, we examined the PL intensity of vitamin C (concentration of 130 µM) in the presence of N-CQDs under varied pH (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) conditions. When comparing different pH conditions, the maximum PL intensity was recorded at pH 7.0; this was the optimum condition for detecting vitamin C.

Additionally, the interaction among the fluorescence intensity for N-CQDs and AA concentration was investigated. The N-CQDs are reduced by AA and the Fig. 4(d) shows the fluorescence quenching for different concentrations of AA ranging from 0 to 130 µM. When the concentration of AA increased, the intensity of PL decreases, showing that AA may significantly diminish the fluorescence of N-CQDs by reducing them. This observation demonstrated the “Turnoff – fluorescence” exhibited in the presence of AA, resulting in non-radiative emission and thus causing the fluorescence quenching. The relation between the fluorescence quenching intensity and the concentrations of AA is given by the equation (F0 - F)/F0 = 0.0067[AA] (μM) − 6.4455 (R2 = 0.9926). Fig. 5 (a) shows the linear relationship among the fluorescence quenching intensity and varying AA concentration from 0 and 130 µM, with an R2 linear correlation coefficient of 0.9926. The LOD was determined to be 0.093 µM using the following equation.
L O D =
3
σ
m

There are two variables in this equation: standard deviation of the samples (σ) and slope of the straight line (m). Table 1 compares the analytical performance parameters such as linear range and detection limit of CQDs in the present work with previous reports and it can be seen that the values are superior or roughly comparable to those previously reported, indicating their potential applicability in biological and environmental systems.

The selectivity of a synthesized N-CQDs toward AA were further determined by examining the effect of several amino acids at the same concentration of 130 µM, including ascorbic acid, glycine, arginine, L-histidine, and cysteine. The fluorescence quenching values [(F0-F)/F0] of N-CQDs containing 130 µM of various amino acids are depicted in Fig. 5(b), the peak intensity of PL inabsence and presence of AAis represented by F0 and F. The result demonstrates that AA is more effective than other amino acids at quenching the fluorescence of N-CQDs. The results suggested sensing platform shows a significantly higher sensitivity to AA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism for AA using fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The process of the quenching of fluorescence for N-CQDs through AA could be described by using the Stern Volmer (S-V) equation in the following manner:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
where the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs in theabsence and presence of AAare represented by theF0 and F; [Q] denotes the concentration of AA; and Ksv represents the kinetic quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer plot reveals a significant linear relation, indicating that this N-CQDs/AA fluorescence sensing system is subject to dynamic quenching.

The fluorescence quenching of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots results from a chemical interaction between the N-CQDs and the AA. There are three main explanations for fluorescence quenching: fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filtering effect (IFE), and photo-induced electron transfer (PET). Depending on the procedure, energy transfer can be classified as either radiated energy transfer or non-radiative energy transfer in FRET. It is possible to split non-radiative energy transfer into two types: FRET, which involves dipole–dipole coupling, and exchange energy transfer, which requires electron exchange. The UV–vis spectra of AA demonstrate a significant absorption band at 285 nm, suggesting that it does not overlap with the emission wavelength of an N-CQDs, which is 435 nm (Fig. 6 (a)). This suggests that the FRET is not followed during the quenching process. In IFE, there is no requirement for considerable spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of AA and the excitation spectrum or the emission spectrum of N-CQDs. Because there is no overlap between these two spectra, IFE is eliminated(Fig. 6(b)).

Thus, electron transport from the excited states of N-CQDs to the unoccupied orbit of AA triggered the fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, hydrogen bonding between N-CQDs and AA may cause aggregation of N-CQDs, which may result in fluorescence quenching. The UV–vis absorption investigation helped to study the interactions between N-CQDs and AA. When N-CQDs and AA are combined, the absorption peak of AA is shifted to 275 nm (Fig. 6(c)). Similarly, the absorption peak of N-CQDs at 277 nm is divided into three weak absorption peaks. These findings suggest that there may be interactions between AA and hydrogen bondingin N-CQDs.

To provide insight on the mechanism behind the quenching of the photoluminescence lifetime of N-CQDs, we investigated the photoluminescence lifetime measurement. In general, the linear Stern-Volmer relations are associated with the occurrence of dynamic and static quenching, as well as quenching of several electronic states. However, the photoluminescence lifetimes of quantum dots vary with dynamic quenching but remain constant with static quenching as a function of AA concentration. Fig. S4 shows the time resolved PL spectra of N-CQDs without and with AA. The average lifetimes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + AA are 9.08 and 8.52 ns, respectively, implying a dynamic quenching process.

As shown in the Fig. S5, the N-CQDs were agglomerated in the presence of AA, due to the strong interaction between the AA and the hydrogen bond in N-CQDs. The agglomerated N-CQDs facilitated the recombination of non-radiative electrons and holes, and then caused the fluorescence quenching. It was verified that the hydrogen bonding between AA and N-CQDs leads to the aggregation by TEM results. Hence, the fluorescence quenching process may be described by the existence of PET and hydrogen-bond interactions.

The N-CQDs were utilized to detect AA in vitamin C tablets to evaluate its sensing cability in a realsample condition. Vitamin C was purchased from a local pharmacy. The real sample analysis was investigated employing a standard addition method at three different concentrations of low (10, 30 µM), medium (70, 90 µM), and high (110, 130 µM) at a pH of 7 (Table 2 ). Under the same conditions, each sample was measured three times (n = 3). The RSD is less than 5%, and its recovery results were within the range of 90.5 – 99.7%, indicating that the proposed N-CQDs can be applied for practical detection of AA in real samples also.

In conclusion, a sustainable and cost-effective approach for producing fluorescent N-CQDs using potato starch as a carbon source has been developed using an ultrasonication-assisted technique. Due to the widespread availability of biomass as a carbon precursor, the precursor becomes cost-effective while also overcoming harmful consequences. The N-CQDs that are produced are spherical in shape and an extremely narrow size distribution of around 3.2 nm, with the quantum yield (QY) was 49.26%. The produced N-CQDs revealed bright blue fluorescencewhen exposed to UV radiation. Under optimal conditions, the interaction between the hydrogen bonds in the N-CQDs and the ascorbic acid (AA) results in electron transfer, resulting in fluorescence quenching. A linear relationship among the fluorescence quenching and varying AA concentration from 0 and 130 µM, with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9926 and also detection limit was 0.093 µM. Additionally, a practical implementation for AA detection in vitamin C tablets was carried out, and the findings revealed a remarkable recovery rate. Hence, this research provides a viable potential for deploying biomass-derived N-doped CQDs as a vitamin C sensor.

M. Preethi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, and Writing -original draft. R. Murugan: Visualization, Formal analysis. C. Viswanathan: Visualization, Validation. N. Ponpandian: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing,Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank DST-FIST, DST-PURSE and UGC-SAP for the instrumental facilities available in the department.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114009.

ce:section.ce:para:
